speaker: interviewer
text: The date of the interview is December 24, 1996. Survivor, Herman Cohn. Interviewer, Elliot Lefkovitz. The city, Chicago. State, Illinois, in the United States. Language of the interview is English. My name is Elliot Lefkovitz. Today's date is December the 24th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Herman Cohn in Chicago, Illinois. The language of the interview will be English. I'd like to begin by asking you to-- you to state your name and to spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Herman Cohn, H-E-R-M-A-N, C-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth and the spelling was the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other names or spellings at all that you have gone by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was born in Germany, the name Herman was spelled with two Ns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I became an American citizen, we deleted one N. So it's-- that's the only difference in my name, from a double N to a single.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: September the 8th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 75 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Essen, in the western part of Germany, also known as Essen an der Ruhr.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's E-S-S-E-N in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Fine. [LAUGHS] What are some of your earliest childhood memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: My earliest childhood memory would probably be the day that my mother passed away. I was five years of age at that time. And for some reason or other, that particular day, and maybe the year prior to her death, I remember my mother. And I remember her, because I used to go shopping with her to the marketplace. She was known to everyone in our family as a very good-hearted, charitable person. She gave away a lot of the food to poor people on the way home from the market. We lived in a town that was a mining town, a suburb of Essen, Essen Katernberg. And she often came home with less than she purchased. I remember that she held me by her hand, and we did-- we went shopping together. And it was an outdoor market. That, I remember. And the day she passed away, I remember, because the entire family came. And there was-- people were screaming, and carrying on, and crying. And they sent me up to my room. And those were my-- really, the earliest memories I have of growing up in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the eldest child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest of-- with this mother, of two children. I have a brother who since passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother-- your mother died in childbirth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother died in childbirth. And also, the baby also did not live. My mother passed away, and the baby passed away. I probably was-- it might have even been stillborn, or lived-- someone said he lived a half an hour, but that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did the birth take place at home, or in a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they rushed her to a hospital. The doctor who had treated her at the time was on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knew that the baby was what was known as a breech birth. The baby was in an awkward situation inside of her. And I believe she bled to death. That's was what I had been told. They rushed her to the hospital. There was a professor who treated her. I even remember his name, Professor Doctor Lerner, in Essen. And this professor was out of town. And some other doctor took over, and just couldn't save her. It was a big, tremendous tragedy, tremendous. She was a young woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else, at the time, was living in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, already, my maternal grandmother lived in the same house with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Your mother's mother. So it-- it must--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, my-- I'm sorry, I-- I made a mistake-- my fraternal, my father's-- my father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother lived with us in the house, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your father was in what sort of business? What sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he took over from his grandfather. He took over a furniture store, which, during my childhood, he operated successfully in a suburb of Essen, called Essen Katernberg. It was called, in German, [GERMAN]. And I say it in German, because some people might watch the tape who are fluent in German and might even remember the name. It was a sizable-- about a two, three-story building with furniture. And he operated that particular store until-- I'm not certain, but about 1932, he decided to get out of it. We had moved already, at that time, out of this house to the neighboring city of Gelsenkirchen. And he purchased a linen-- like a linen jobbing, manufacturing place. And that was-- he had that business until he left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far back in Germany can you trace your-- your father's family roots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly until the 1800s, I have records of where the family came from. Yes, mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my dad met his mother-- his wife, my mother, while he was in the German army, stationed in the Balkans. And on a Friday evening, he was invited to the home of the Stein family for a Shabbat dinner. And there was this young woman. Her name was Ilonka [? Ilu ?] Stein. And that's how he met my mother. And he obviously fell in love with her. And when the war was over, he went back to Hungary. And the two of them then had a civil marriage in Vienna. They were married in Vienna, immediately after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they came from there to-- to Essen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then, my mother and dad moved to Essen. And as I said, he then-- from that point on, he began to operate the furniture store.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war, your father was in the-- the air corps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he ended up as a-- in the German air corps, so to speak. But in the beginning, he was just in the German army. I'm not certain what branch of the service, but he-- when he was inducted into the German army, he was probably just in the infantry or artillery, or one of the ordinary services. But for-- somehow, he ended up as a flier in the Balkans. I-- as I said, in my-- somewhere, I have a picture of him standing next to an airplane. And he explained to me that he was, indeed, with the German army in the-- in the German air corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't recall him being a pilot? More in support staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't believe he was a pilot. I think he might have been a co-pilot, or a bombardier, or something else that people do when they fly war planes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a German patriot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and how. Very much so. All his life he-- he and all his brothers were inducted into the service. He lost one brother, got killed during the war. And yes, they were all very much-- very patriotic Germans. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that-- did he pass that patriotism on to you? Were you also-- do you recall, also, as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think we were always raised first as Germans, and secondly as Jews, very much like most of the children here are raised first as-- at least most of them, not all of them-- are raised first as patriotic Americans, and then Jewish. And we were raised, first of all, as patriotic Germans, and secondly Jewish. That's how we were raised. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father belonged to a veterans organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was like the Jewish War Veterans, they call it, RJF, [GERMAN]. That was always mentioned in my home. And like I said, he was very, very German in that regard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he win any medals at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he with him, I think it was called an [GERMAN], I believe, he won. He might have had others, too, but I remember that particular medal he received for whatever he did during the war. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Iron Cross Second Class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know that. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. As far as the-- the Jewish component in your family, do you remember any holidays with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. As I said, we might have been Germans first, but we always belonged to a synagogue. We always attended services, especially on all the major holidays. And we-- we-- we were raised Jewish, and we observed the holidays. We had Pesach, Passover services, Seder. We-- we certainly-- my folks got all dressed up for the high holidays. My dad, I remember quite well, always wore a-- a high hat, a top hat. And very elegant, he looked on the holidays. And yes, we were-- we were raised Jewish, no doubt, in the-- in the German, liberal way, rather than in an orthodox way, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the-- the-- the German liberal way meant that-- was-- did you-- did you have a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not have a kosher home. We did not have any-- as I remember, we never had any-- any pig, ham, bacon, or food of that nature in our home. But other than that, we did not have a kosher home. We had a challah every Friday night. There was a gentleman in our town who baked the challahs, who delivered the challahs every Friday night, and lit candles on Shabbat. That was about the size of our Jewishness. Of course, I was bar mitzvah and had many Jewish friends. But I also had many Gentile friends. We grew up in a neighborhood-- there was-- there was no-- no such thing in my town as a Jewish neighborhood. We grew up wherever people could afford to live, which is probably somewhat what's happening these days. That's where you lived. And I was surrounded in-- in a-- in a community with many Gentiles, and therefore, had as many Gentile friends, prior to Hitler, as I had Jewish friends. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitism, prior to Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Absolutely not. I cannot think of one single incident of anti-Semitism, as far as I was concerned. There might have been some, but I-- I don't remember any of it, until after Hitler came to power. That's when-- it was just someone turned a key, and things changed dramatically, almost from the-- January 1933 on, from the very beginning of Hitler's takeover. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go-- you went to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Initially, I went to a public grammar school, when we lived in Essen Katernberg. But then, in my first or second year of school, my parents decided, as I said, to move to the town-- city of Gelsenkirchen, which was practically adjacent to Essen Katernberg, maybe approximately eight miles, at the most. And there, I entered a-- a Jewish day school, a Jewish school-- [GERMAN] was the name of it, and until I was old enough to go to high school, a German high school. That was called [GERMAN]. And I went there through classes, [GERMAN], about almost five years of high school, practically the equivalent of a-- an American high school education. And that lasted until the middle '30s, when relatives who had arrived in the United States and lived in the city of Chicago wrote to my parents and said it would be advisable to take Herman out of school, so he would learn a trade. Because if he get-- comes to the United States, eventually, hopefully-- which were our intentions already in the mid '30s-- that he would have-- immediately have a job. And if he learns a trade, he will have a job. And if he doesn't have a trade, its most difficult to find employment. So my parents decided to take me out of the German high school. And I became apprentice-- an apprentice in a tailor shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by this time, your father had remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So that's the period in his life he remarried. I believe it might have been 1928. And my-- our stepmother-- well, for a while, our grandmother, our fraternal grandmother, what lived with us, raised both my brother and me, with the-- with the help of a-- a governess. And my father then decided to remarry. And our stepmother-- her name was Theresa-- she-- she then continued to raise us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And relations were-- were good?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not. Initially, I think, they were pretty good. My grandmother even continued to live with us for a while. However, I believe my stepmother, especially when she and my dad had another boy-- and this little boy, wonderful little boy by the name of Helmut, died of natural causes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a-- as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was five or six years old. And that made my mother very bitter. It made her-- made her bitter towards my brother, Walter, and myself, because she lost her child. Her child was lost. And we, who were her stepchildren, continued to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a difficult childhood. I didn't analyze it in those days. I just know that we got probably hit more often than other children, surely some of it deservedly so. But there was an excessive amount of punishment. There was also very little love in the home. So my childhood was not an easy one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized it more as I got older. I realized it even more so than when my dad first got married. And that's the way it was, until the last years of her life, when we lived in the United States, my stepmother mellowed. And we had a much better relationship during the latter years of her life than we had while we were being raised as-- as children and-- and teenagers. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a close relationship with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like all brothers, we-- we fought. I was the older. He was the younger. I never wanted to take him along when I went somewhere. And I think that's rather typical of-- of-- of siblings. So-- but we got along. I mean, we had our fights, but we got along.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of an extended family was there, for example, on holiday celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wonderful. That was a wonderful part of our family. My father's family lived all around us. His brothers and his sisters all married within a short distance of where we lived. They did not all live in Gelsenkirchen, but they didn't live all that far away. And since my dad's mother, my grandma-- who later was deported-- my grandma lived with us, all the family came to visit, usually on weekends, usually on Sundays. And we had like an open house. There were so many of aunts, and uncles, and cousins, who visited at our home, out home was like-- like I just said, an open house. People came, and we had coffee and cake on a Sunday afternoon. And we celebrated birthdays together, especially the birthdays of my-- my grandmother. It was always a big, festive occasion when everyone came. And we children had to recite-- recite poetry. And I remember those days quite well. We used to play games. [CLEARS THROAT] Originally, in the furniture store, we played games called hide and seek and as children, with all the cousins. So I have a fond memory of-- of that period of my life. When the family got together, it was always very, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did they get together also for Passover Seders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some of us did. Some of us, who lived in the same town got together, yes. We had Seder in my uncle Louie's house many times, because he was more observant than we were. And he could conduct a Seder. I mean, he really could conduct Seder. And we sat through it, diligently. And he did an excellent job. And so I remember those Seders quite well, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the-- the education that you received in the Jewish day school, do you remember anything, any outstanding teachers, or any incidents, or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Fraulein Goldblatt. She was a teacher. Lera Katz was another teacher. I mention their name, because we referred to them in that manner, Miss Goldblatt and Teacher Katz, two of them. There might have been others, but I remember those two quite well. And they were fine. They were good teachers. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably used to hit us now and then, which was standard for-- for European education, certainly, German education. If you-- if there was an infraction of the rules, or you did something that they perceived to be incorrect in their eyes, bam, wham. I mean, we got it. And I especially did. I probably was not the most best-behaved boy in class, I imagine. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your bar mitzvah was in Gelsenkirchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah was in Gelsenkirchen. Dr. Gallinen was the rabbi. He-- he was a wonderful rabbi. And [INAUDIBLE] Shul, was his name, he taught me my bar mitzvah lessons. And I went through the entire process. I had to learn how to lay tefillin and the whole bit, something I didn't do at home. But I had to learn all these things before I became bar mitzvah. And it wasn't the kind of bar mitzvah that the children here are used to, believe me. I could only-- it was a real bar mitzvah. I could only invite a small circle of friends. And it was a family party, a very small family gathering, not the elaborate festivities that they stage today, when-- when children become bar mitzvah. I think it was more meaningful, from a religious point of view, than our bar mitzvahs are today, at least, the ones that we have attended so many times. The language, of course, that you spoke at home, was German-- only German. Only German. Only German. That was the only language. Absolutely. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Yiddish-- how-- how was Yiddish regarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no Yiddish at all in our surroundings, except there was some immigration from Eastern Europe into my hometown, mostly from Poland. And these people spoke Yiddish, before they also-- in the end, they spoke as fluent German as we did. But when they came originally from Poland, they spoke Yiddish, which we could understand. It was more like a German Yiddish, not like some other Yiddish that sounds somewhat different. And we could understand them, but we didn't socialize with them. We did-- there was a-- a gulf between the Eastern European and the German Jews. And I felt that. I didn't like it already as a child, but it was something that was inborn, probably, in our parents. There was-- it was-- it has to be there, for the record. I must say that. I-- I didn't like it at all, because I befriended children in school whose parents came from Poland. And we were good friends. And I couldn't see-- I couldn't-- to me, there was no difference between myself and some of these kids who came from Eastern Europe. But our parents, they did-- they were prejudiced. That's all I can tell you. And it was a sad chapter, and as far as I'm concerned, in the life of German Jewry. It should have never been, but it was. That's why I tell you this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were you from the French border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, fairly far. We were closer to the Dutch border, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Closer to the Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mm-hm. Oh, absolutely, yeah-- Dutch and Belgian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- did you-- did you visit the Netherlands, or any other country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several times, because some of my relatives lived in Holland. But the-- the ones who lived in Holland, originally, in Germany, they married Dutch citizens. And then, they were among the first ones to leave Germany and then continued to live in Holland. Unfortunately, they-- some of them didn't get out. And some of them were in hiding during the war, in Holland. And there some-- were some wonderful Dutch Gentile families. As a matter of fact, in Jerusalem, they have an Avenue of the Righteous. And some of these people, who saved my family, where inducted in this Avenue of the Righteous, in Jerusalem. So they were survivors, but not all of them were survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was specific-- who was-- specifically, who was saved? This was from your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were all relatives. These were sisters of my dad, and their families, and the children, my-- my cousins, that were saved. And then, late during the war, I might as well mention, since we talk about that period of time, when we were able to get out-- and we'll come back to that-- my grandmother, the one I spoke about earlier, she was fortunate enough to get out of Germany, to Holland, to live with her daughters. However, she was then, after Germany occupied Holland, she was caught in one of the many raids. And she was deported. And to the best of my knowledge, she was, of course, killed in a-- in a camp, either near Riga or in Sobibor. Somewhere, there are records to show where my grandmother was killed. But the Nazis killed my grandmother. At the age of 84 is when they picked her up and-- and deported her. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the coming to power of Hitler. Before Hitler and the Nazis came to power, you were acquainted with the Nazi movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because there were riots in the streets of Gelsenkirchen. There was a very strong Communist and Social Democrat movement. There were a lot of mine workers who were members of the Communist Party. And the Nazis opposed the Communists. And they had street brawls and fights. And I witnessed some of those. And I also witnessed when these-- the stormtroopers, as well as the Communists, went marching down the streets of our town with flags, and banners, and what have you. That was in the early 1930s, so I was very much aware of the Nazi movement coming online. But we didn't-- we were-- we were not all that concerned, as far as-- certainly, our family was concerned. My dad always said, well, these movements, oh, they come and they go, like so many did during the Weimar Republic, which was the period after World War I. One regime after another came and fell. So they all thought this would be just a-- a glitch, really. But it turned out to be otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your parents continued to vote for the Social Democrats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Yes, they were Social Democrats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once the Nazis came to power, what-- you were-- you were, at that time, in Gelsenkirchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, at that time, were in the-- the Jewish day school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- in 1933-- I'm tracing back-- was about the time when I might have transferred from the Jewish day school to the gymnasium that I mentioned. And I'd have to think about it for a minute. 1933-- no, I think I was already enrolled in the [GERMAN] at that time, in-- in Gelsenkirchen, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gentile boys that I had befriended, or the school-- my school chums, certainly showed an alienation towards all of us Jewish boys. Almost from the very beginning, they just fell in line with the rest of the German people, because Hitler, from day one, advocated an anti-Jewish trend. And-- and he-- his speeches all were anti-Semitic speeches. So the general population followed him, like a-- you know, a bunch of sheep. And-- and the-- and the young people did the very same thing. And-- and they called us names on the street and-- and picked fights with us. We got beaten up. That was the very beginning. And even some of my closer Gentile friends just simply didn't associate was-- with-- with-- with us anymore. They-- they stayed away. Just-- That was the very first inclination or indication we had of what was about to happen to the rest of us in-- in the years to come. But in the very beginning, it was just overt anti-Semitism that we read about, we heard about on the radio, Hitler's speeches. And the kids picked it up. And they just acted like Hitler did. And of course, then, it began to get worse in school. And--
